rationales.achievement: He centers the review as "exactly what I've been working toward for two years" and immediately starts planning the next milestone, showing strong focus on excelling and advancing.
rationales.power: He is already calculating how a senior manager promotion leads to the "director track," which points to a clear pull toward higher rank and authority.

rationales.hedonism: He says he "didn't sleep much" in order to keep the project on track, sacrificing comfort and immediate wellbeing for work.
rationales.achievement: He "hit all deadlines anyway" despite covering two sick teammates' work, and notes that the "Director noticed," signaling strong performance under pressure.
He is preoccupied with being a "strong candidate" and insists that "excellent work" should count, showing strong investment in competence and recognition.
rationales.power: The entry revolves around competing for a promotion and realizing that "high-profile projects" and visibility affect who rises, showing concern with status and influence.

rationales.hedonism: He has been "in the office until 9pm every day this week" and gives up leisure and rest during the holiday period for work.
rationales.achievement: He frames staying in Dubai as "more time to prove myself," showing that career advancement is still driving the decision.
rationales.security: He says leaving for the engagement "feels like a risk I can't take," explicitly prioritizing safety and career stability over travel.
rationales.benevolence: He chooses to miss his sister's engagement and dismisses it as "just one event," putting a close family relationship behind work concerns.

rationales.security: He emphasizes that his "visa is tied to [his] employment" and that losing the job means "30 days to find another or leave," showing strong attention to precarity and stability.
rationales.benevolence: He remains physically absent from the engagement party and recognizes that Layla wanted him there "in person," reflecting a failure to show up for close family.

rationales.hedonism: He describes living with "head down, eyes on the goal" and not looking around because the "periphery is full of things that will slow you down," which rejects present enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: He says "Get the promotion, obviously" and frames his life around staying focused on that goal.
rationales.security: When asked what he is surviving, he answers "The uncertainty. The feeling that nothing is really mine," showing that stability and assurance are central concerns.

rationales.achievement: He accepts the feedback and signs up for the presentation slot, treating it as part of what it takes to advance rather than staying comfortable.
rationales.power: He embraces "strategic visibility" and public presentation because senior leadership requires being seen, which directly serves greater influence and authority.
rationales.benevolence: He books a "non-refundable" flight home for Mama's birthday because he realizes work "can't be the only thing that matters," prioritizing a close family relationship.

rationales.achievement: He successfully delivers the all-hands presentation and values that others "compliment[ed] the insights," showing satisfaction in strong performance.
rationales.power: The presentation increases his visibility across the organization, and the shift from seeing Ahmad as only a threat reflects a changed stance toward workplace status competition.

rationales.hedonism: He chooses a celebratory dinner and says, "Tonight, I'm going to let myself feel this," explicitly permitting present enjoyment instead of deferring it.
rationales.achievement: He treats the promotion as the payoff for "Two years of work" and "all those late nights, the stress, the sacrifices," making success and recognition central.
rationales.power: The new role brings "a team of twelve direct reports instead of five," indicating movement into greater authority and control over others.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the district's binder and starts designing "a unit where students write and perform original one-act plays," showing a strong drive to teach on his own terms.
rationales.stimulation: The wilderness guide posting stays open in his browser, and he is drawn to work outside the dead routine of repeated PD slides and scripted lessons.
rationales.conformity: He wants to "throw" the standard binder and recalls that the best class last spring was a mock trial he "snuck in," openly resisting prescribed norms.

rationales.stimulation: He misses the version of himself who "woke up not knowing what the day would look like," showing a clear pull toward novelty and unpredictability.
rationales.security: He says he "traded the unknown for the predictable" and is no longer sure that was the right choice, signaling rejection of stability as a guiding good.

rationales.self_direction: He "threw out" the lesson plan mid-class and replaced it with a format that matched his own teaching instincts rather than the prescribed script.
rationales.stimulation: He describes the class as "messy and loud" with students physically moving and debating, and that intensity is exactly what makes the work feel alive again.
rationales.conformity: He defines the meaningful version of the job against "the binder" and admits that teaching this way means pushing through "cracks" in the system rather than following it.
rationales.benevolence: He centers a quieter student saying it was the "first time he didn't watch the clock all period," showing genuine care for what helps his students engage.

rationales.stimulation: He is preoccupied with "a six-month wilderness guide contract in Arizona" and feels drawn to a more open-ended, less mapped life.
rationales.security: He says that "mapping out the next five years makes me feel like I can't breathe," which directly rejects long-term predictability and settled plans.

rationales.self_direction: What attracts him is a class that is "student-directed, no textbook, no standardized test alignment," matching his preference for autonomous, self-authored work.
rationales.conformity: He resists being "locked in" by committee obligations and structured meetings, and questions whether the whole career is the wrong "container" for how he wants to work.

rationales.tradition: He lingers on the old Fenix warehouse, Mam, Tante Lien, and the feeling that "the bones are there but the life has changed," showing strong orientation toward continuity with the past.
He contrasts app-controlled comfort with his grandmother's house, saying "You can't curate warmth," and values the lived, inherited way of home life over a simulated version.

rationales.conformity: He stays for coffee with the old woman even though it makes him "fifteen minutes late" and gets him a warning from the app, choosing personal judgment over imposed expectations.
rationales.tradition: He frames "solid ground" through his grandfather surviving the flood and his father keeping the shop, explicitly measuring his life against inherited family patterns.
rationales.benevolence: He accepts the old woman's invitation because she "had nobody to drink it with" and values that human connection more than the next delivery.

rationales.tradition: He misses Koningsdag, a recurring family ritual marked by "Opa's flag on the pole like always," and explicitly regrets giving that up.
rationales.benevolence: He says, "My niece asked where I was" and "I should have been there," showing he failed to show up for close family in favor of work.

rationales.security: He says his body knows how to rest in the house because "everything is just where it's been for thirty years," linking peace directly to familiarity and predictability.
rationales.tradition: The visit revolves around Mam's kitchen, church-group cake, Dad's old spring ritual with the awning, and "the shape of a day" he recognizes from childhood.
rationales.benevolence: He drives to Zeeland specifically to spend the day with Mam, sharing coffee, cake, and time together rather than staying absorbed in work.

rationales.security: He envies that his father "knew where he'd be every day for the next ten years" and says his own life feels like "scattered dots," showing a desire for steadiness and structure.
rationales.tradition: He values routines that are shared and handed down, saying his father's patterns mattered because "the whole family moved through them together."
When Bram asks him to say grace and later says, "Oma would have liked it," the moment clearly honors an inherited family and religious practice.
rationales.benevolence: His prayer centers gratitude for Bram being "healthy and here with us," and he experiences the dinner as a meaningful act of care within the family.

rationales.power: He stresses that "there's no negotiation" and "no union rep to call," ending with "just an app you log into," which highlights a stark lack of influence or control.
rationales.security: What angers him is losing a "steady amount" while needing this work because "This is how I pay rent," showing clear priority on stable income.
rationales.tradition: He measures the situation against his father's handshake ethic and the belief that if "a man's word doesn't hold," you walk away, grounding his judgment in inherited standards.

rationales.security: He says that knowing this "ground" is there keeps him from feeling "completely adrift," showing that continuity and predictability provide emotional safety.
rationales.tradition: The entire entry is anchored in inherited practices and symbols: the old church in Goes, the same family pew, saying grace, "the language," and Mam setting the table "the same way every Sunday."
